design_philosophy.core_loop: Progress through side-scrolling stages by defeating waves of enemies and bosses; unlock new martial arts moves via experience system to overcome increasingly difficult encounters
design_philosophy.primary_tension: Resource management (health, time limits) balanced against steady power progression; two-player dynamic shifts from arcade co-op to alternating turns in NES adaptation
design_philosophy.player_fantasy: Playing as martial artist Billy Lee (or Jimmy) rescuing girlfriend Marian from Black Warriors gang; mastering combat through earned move unlocks rather than raw execution
design_philosophy.what_makes_it_opinionated: NES version deliberately deviates from arcade formula by introducing EXP-based move unlocks rather than relying on arcade's pattern memorization; Mode B (one-on-one fighting) offers distinct gameplay philosophy separate from story mode; game prioritizes progression fantasy over arcade-style score chasing

difficulty.lives_system: Multiple extra lives with life gauge that depletes on hits; loss occurs when life gauge or timer reaches zero
difficulty.checkpointing: Stage-based progression with automatic transitions between major areas; no mid-stage checkpoints
difficulty.player_agency_in_difficulty: EXP system allows player to strengthen character by unlocking new moves, mitigating difficulty through power progression rather than pure execution skill; Mode B offers separate difficulty tuning where CPU opponents have extra health (notably Abobo)

progression.upgrade_system: Experience-based move unlock system unique to NES version; players unlock new martial arts techniques (throws, elbow strikes) as they gain levels, differentiating from arcade's static moveset
progression.unlock_mechanism: Experience points earned through enemy defeat drive character progression; higher levels unlock advanced combat moves
replayability_hooks: Mode B one-on-one fighting with player-versus-player capability
Character selection affecting gameplay (different sprite sizes and move sets)
Speedrunning community evidenced by active leaderboards with times ranging 9-35 minutes
Multiple regional versions with slight differences

world_building_techniques: Continuous side-scrolling world establishing sense of progression through defined locations (city slum → factory → forest → gang hideout)
Character-driven plot (rescue Marian from Black Warriors)
Cinematic cutscenes modeled on arcade version inspiration
Unused staff roll sequence suggests planned ending cinematics (restored in fan hacks)

boss_design.philosophy: Boss encounters serve as stage transitions and difficulty gates; designed to require mastery of accumulated move unlocks from progression system
notable_bosses: Chin (end of Stage 2, positioned at construction site top; has unused ladder-climbing animation)
Abobo (notable as most dangerous CPU opponent in Mode B due to excessive health scaling; was planned to have 'atomic throw' move from arcade version)
Willy (final boss in hideout, brother conflict narrative)

faction_indicators: Black Warriors gang as antagonist faction controlling territory
Billy and Jimmy Lee as protagonist duo contrasted against gang
Two-player mechanic creates explicit team dynamic (co-op in arcade; alternating turns in NES)
danger_signals: Gang territory (city slum) as hostile environment requiring navigation
Boss characters (Abobo, Chin, Willy) serve as power-tier indicators
Weapon availability (baseball bats, whips, throwing knives, dynamite) signals escalating threat levels
Environmental hazards (spikes, holes, rivers, traps in hideout) mark danger zones
environmental_storytelling: Stage progression through distinct locations tells story of infiltrating gang territory
Slum → Factory → Forest → Hideout arc reflects journey from public spaces into criminal infrastructure
Hideout filled with traps conveys organized crime sophistication
Unused dragon-head statue (planned breakable object) suggests mystical gang symbolism
